failure_surface: Incorrect row indexing when writing data to new columns caused the first data row to be incorrectly handled, resulting in output mismatch in columns H:I across the specified range H1:I10.
root_cause: The agent wrote headers to row 1 (H1, I1) but then iterated starting from row 1 for data processing. In openpyxl, if row 1 contains headers, data rows begin at row 2. Starting the loop at row 1 meant either skipping the first actual data row or treating the header row as a data row.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: After placing column headers in row 1, start data iteration from row 2 to ensure all data rows are correctly processed without conflating headers with data.
evidence: Agent code sets headers: ws['H1'] = 'Debits' and ws['I1'] = 'Credits'
Agent then loops: for row_num in range(1, last_row + 1) — starts at row 1, which is the header row
Answer position specifies H1:I10, implying 1 header row plus up to 9 data rows need modification
Output verification shows 10 total rows processed via print(f"Total rows processed: {last_row}")
Sheet-Level Manipulation type requires modifications across entire worksheet range, not just specific cells

memory_items.title: Header vs. Data Row Distinction in openpyxl
memory_items.lesson: When writing new headers to row 1 using openpyxl, always start data processing loops from row 2 to avoid treating the header row as a data row.
memory_items.generalization: Confirm whether row 1 contains existing headers before iterating; adjust loop boundaries accordingly to prevent header/data row confusion.

memory_items.title: Verify Target Range Coverage Before Processing
memory_items.lesson: Before iterating over rows, verify the expected row count matches the answer_position range to ensure complete coverage of required data rows.
memory_items.generalization: Cross-check worksheet max_row against instruction-specified ranges; add validation to confirm all target rows will be touched by the loop.

memory_items.title: Row Indexing Consistency Across Libraries
memory_items.lesson: openpyxl uses 1-based row indexing while pandas uses 0-based DataFrame indexing; mixing these conventions during cell operations leads to off-by-one errors.
memory_items.generalization: When combining pandas and openpyxl, explicitly convert between their row/column indexing systems or consistently use one library's paradigm for cell access.

edits.content:
## Additional Lessons

### Row Indexing Safety
- When writing headers to row 1 with openpyxl, begin data row iteration from row 2.
- Always validate that loop boundaries (start/end) cover the full answer_position range without overlapping header rows.
- Be explicit about 1-based (openpyxl) versus 0-based (pandas) indexing when switching between libraries within the same workflow.

### Range Verification Protocol
- Before executing row-wise transformations, print the detected max_row and compare against the task's answer_position specification.
- Add a sanity check that confirms every required data row falls within the iteration range.
